title: A land with a long memory
dek: Palestine’s story did not begin with the latest war. It is the story of ancient towns, many faiths, Ottoman districts, the British Mandate and a modern people whose life remains rooted in the land.
imageAlt: Map of Mandatory Palestine in 1946 showing major cities
quote: Palestine is not an empty stage. It is a place of layered history and continuous human belonging.
paragraphs: The land between the Mediterranean and the Jordan has carried many names and many peoples. Canaanite cities, Jewish kingdoms, Roman and Byzantine rule, Islamic dynasties, Crusader states and the Ottoman Empire all left traces. Muslim, Christian and Jewish histories belong in an honest account; none makes Palestinian life disposable.

For four centuries, until the First World War, the area formed part of the Ottoman Empire. Towns including Al-Quds, Yafa, Haifa, Nablus, Al-Khalil, Akka, Safad and Gaza were connected by trade, pilgrimage, family and farming. Arabic-speaking Muslims and Christians formed the large majority in the late Ottoman and Mandate eras, alongside long-established Jewish communities and later Jewish immigration.

After the Ottoman defeat, the League of Nations placed Palestine under British administration in 1922. British policy, including the 1917 Balfour Declaration, intensified competing national projects while Palestinians sought independence and representative government.
A 1946 map is not a claim that history stopped in that year. It is evidence of a named, inhabited country of cities, villages, roads and relationships immediately before the Nakba transformed it.
Today, historical honesty means holding every layer of the land’s past while refusing the erasure of the Palestinian people who call it home.
sources: UN — History of the Question of Palestine

title: Homes, keys and the unfinished story of return
dek: In 1948, more than 750,000 Palestinians were uprooted. Hundreds of villages were depopulated or destroyed. The key became a promise that home would not be forgotten.
imageAlt: Palestinian families leaving Jaffa in 1948 with belongings in carts
imageCredit: UNRWA Photo Archive via Wikimedia Commons
quote: The Nakba is not only a date. It is the continuing separation of people from homes, land and return.
paragraphs: Nakba means catastrophe. During the 1948 war, more than 750,000 Palestinians were uprooted from their homes, many forcibly, according to the United Nations. Families fled or were expelled from cities and villages carrying what they could, expecting danger to pass.

Hundreds of Palestinian villages were depopulated or destroyed. In Yafa, Lydda, Ramle, Haifa, Safad and across the countryside, a social world was fractured. Property, orchards, shops, family papers and neighbourhoods were lost or placed beyond reach.

The house key became a Palestinian symbol because dispossession is intimate. A key remembers a door, a courtyard, a fig tree, the room where bread cooled and the path a child took to school. Descendants inherit these details even when they cannot visit.

UN General Assembly resolution 194 said refugees wishing to return and live at peace should be permitted to do so at the earliest practicable date, with compensation for those choosing not to return and for lost or damaged property. Its implementation remains unresolved.
Remembering the Nakba does not deny anyone else’s suffering. It insists that Palestinian loss be named, documented and answered with rights rather than treated as an inconvenient preface.
facts: UN resolution addressing return and compensation

title: Palestinian life is more than survival
dek: From tatreez stitches and dabke steps to coffee, classrooms, stone houses and market streets, Palestinian culture is living—not a museum label and not only a record of loss.
imageAlt: Olive harvesters in Qafin in the northern West Bank
quote: To describe Palestine only through catastrophe is another kind of erasure. People also love, cook, study, joke, build and celebrate.
paragraphs: Palestinian life changes from the coast to the Galilee, from Al-Quds and Bethlehem to Nablus, Al-Khalil, Jenin and Gaza. Local accents, recipes and embroidery patterns carry geography. A dress can encode a village; a song can preserve a road.

Tatreez, traditional Palestinian embroidery, is practiced across generations. Patterns once signalled place, status and occasion; today they also carry national memory through dresses, cushions, art and contemporary fashion. UNESCO inscribed the art on its Representative List of Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2021.

Food maps the seasons: maqluba turned onto a communal platter, musakhan rich with sumac and olive oil, knafeh associated with Nablus, bread from a taboon, zaatar, lentils, grapes, figs and citrus. These are daily practices before they are symbols.

Palestinian society includes Muslims and Christians, villagers, city families, Bedouin communities, refugees and a wide diaspora. No single portrait contains all of it. Universities, theatres, newspapers, music, craft and technology belong beside agriculture and heritage.

Occupation, checkpoints, blockade, displacement and economic restrictions shape daily life, but they do not define its whole meaning. Palestinian culture survives because it is continually made, shared and changed by living people.

title: Al-Quds is the capital of Palestine
dek: Palestinians claim القدس as their capital. International law does not recognize Israel’s unilateral annexation of East Jerusalem, which the United Nations treats as occupied Palestinian territory.
imageAlt: The Dome of the Rock with the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in the background in Al-Quds
imageCredit: Berthold Werner / Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA
quote: Our editorial position is clear: East Jerusalem—Al-Quds—is the capital of Palestine, and exclusive Israeli sovereignty over the whole city is not internationally recognized.
paragraphs: Al-Quds is central to Palestinian political, religious and cultural life. The Old City holds Al-Aqsa Mosque and the Dome of the Rock, the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, the Western Wall and neighbourhoods whose families have lived there for generations.

Israel declares Jerusalem its capital and, after occupying East Jerusalem in 1967, extended its law and administration there. But UN Security Council Resolution 478 rejected Israel’s 1980 Basic Law seeking to alter the city’s status and called on states not to recognize it.

The State of Palestine’s 1988 Declaration of Independence proclaimed Jerusalem as its capital. In the prevailing UN framework, East Jerusalem is part of the occupied Palestinian territory. Its final political arrangement remains disputed, but unilateral annexation does not settle sovereignty under international law.

For Palestinians, the issue is lived through residency rules, settlement expansion, home demolitions, family separation, access to worship and pressure on neighbourhoods such as Sheikh Jarrah and Silwan. A capital is not just a flag over institutions; it is the right of a people to remain and govern their civic life.

Al-Quds can be sacred to multiple peoples without Palestinian rights being subordinated. A just future must protect every holy place and every resident while recognizing East Jerusalem as the Palestinian capital.

title: The house, the olive and the fig
dek: A demolished house and an uprooted tree are not only damaged objects. They can sever a family’s shelter, income, inheritance and physical claim to place.
imageAlt: A Palestinian family harvesting olives near Jenin
quote: The olive tree says: we tended this land before us, and we intend our children to tend it after us.
paragraphs: Olive trees are food, income and inheritance. Harvest is family labour: sheets spread beneath branches, ladders balanced against old trunks, meals shared in the grove. Fig trees grow beside homes and terraces, giving shade and fruit without needing ceremony.

That ordinary continuity is why attacks on land carry such force. OCHA documented 178 olive-harvest-related settler attacks between 1 October and 30 November 2025, involving casualties, property damage, stolen crops or equipment and vandalized trees. In Al Mughayyir, OCHA reported that Israeli bulldozers uprooted thousands of olive trees in August 2025.

Homes face another pressure. Israeli authorities frequently demolish Palestinian structures in the West Bank, including East Jerusalem, for lacking building permits that OCHA says are nearly impossible for Palestinians to obtain. In 2025, more than 1,700 Palestinians were displaced by such demolitions—the highest annual total since OCHA began this documentation in 2009.

When a family loses a house, the loss extends beyond walls. It can mean debt, school disruption, documents buried in rubble, separation from neighbours and the weakening of a community’s presence on its land.

The olive and fig are not romantic substitutes for political rights. Protecting groves, homes and access to land requires accountability, an end to forcible displacement and equal protection of Palestinian property.
facts: Olive trees uprooted in Al Mughayyir

title: Gaza, genocide and the duty to protect
dek: Children, women, men and older people have been killed on a devastating scale. Major human-rights bodies and a UN commission have concluded that Israel is committing genocide; the ICJ case remains pending on the merits.
imageAlt: Palestinians inspect destroyed homes in Gaza City in October 2023
imageCredit: Naaman Omar / APAimages via Wikimedia Commons
quote: No political objective can make the destruction of a people acceptable. Palestinian civilian life must be protected now.
paragraphs: By early December 2025, Gaza’s Ministry of Health reported more than 70,000 Palestinians killed; OCHA cited identified deaths including at least 20,179 children, 10,427 women and 4,813 older people. The categories overlap and do not capture every victim, but they show the civilian devastation in unmistakable terms.

Amnesty International concluded in December 2024 that Israel was committing genocide against Palestinians in Gaza. In September 2025, the UN Independent International Commission of Inquiry also concluded that Israeli authorities and forces had committed and continued to commit genocide. Israel rejects the genocide allegation and says its military campaign targets Hamas.

South Africa’s genocide case against Israel remains before the International Court of Justice. The Court ordered provisional measures to protect rights under the Genocide Convention and enable humanitarian assistance. Those orders are legally significant, but they are not a final judgment on whether genocide occurred.

Legal precision should not become an excuse for moral delay. The killing of civilians, destruction of homes and hospitals, mass displacement, deprivation of food and medicine, and damage to the conditions necessary for life demand protection, ceasefire, aid, investigation and accountability.

Standing with Gaza means centering its people rather than treating them as a demographic obstacle. Children, women, men and older people are not collateral scenery. They have names, families and equal claims to safety, freedom and a future in Palestine.
facts: Palestinians reported killed by early Dec 2025
Identified children among deaths cited by OCHA
